feedback.text: I am writing to express strong support for the Stop Killing Games initiative and to urge lawmakers to ensure the Digital Fairness Act includes robust provisions that protect consumers' rights to access and preserve legally purchased digital contentespecially video games. As it currently stands, game publishers have the power to remotely disable games, even when consumers have paid full price for them. This is not only unfair but undermines consumer trust and violates the spirit of ownership. When someone buys a game, they should have the right to play it indefinitely, even if online services are later shut down. The Stop Killing Games campaign highlights a critical gap in digital rights. Games are not just products; they are cultural artifacts and works of art. Shutting them down permanently deprives people of enjoyment, access to media theyve paid for, and in many cases, access to personal progress or memories tied to those games. It's akin to a publisher recalling books from shelves or deleting purchased films from home collections. I urge you to include the following protections in the Digital Fairness Act: Right to Offline Access: Require that games which are no longer supported online be patched or preserved in a way that allows continued offline play. Consumer Ownership Protections: Ensure that digital purchases come with rights akin to physical ownership, including the right to retain access after official support ends. Preservation and Archiving Rights: Protect the ability of individuals and preservation groups to maintain and distribute older, unsupported games for historical and personal use. Including such measures will not only protect consumers but also preserve digital culture for future generations. Thank you for taking this feedback into consideration. Sincerely, Iksan Havziji Supporter of the Stop Killing Games initiative
